The Index This section is a brief overview of the Index.
It contains a summary of the principal features of the Index and is not a complete description of the Index.
As at the date of this Prospectus, the summary of the Index in this section is accurate and consistent with the complete description of the Index.
Complete information on the Index appears in the websit e identified below.
Such information may change from time to time and details of the changes will appear on that website.

General Information on the Index The Index is designed to represent the performance of securities from the gold mining industry listed on the SEHK, the New York Stock Exchange, Nasdaq Stock Exchange, NYSE American Stock Exchange, Australian Securities Exchange, Toronto Stock Exchange or TSX Venture Exchange (excluding the SEHK, collectively the “International Stock Exchanges”).
The Index is a net total return index, i.e.
the performance of the Index reflects the reinvestment of dividends and distributions, net of withholding tax, from the Index constituents .
The Index is market capitalisation weighted index.
The Index is denominated and quoted in HKD.
The Index was launched on 26 September 2025 and had a base level of 1,000 on 17 March 2023.
As of 31 December 2025, the Index had a total market capitalisation of approximately HKD4727.49billion and 30 constituents.
Index Provider The Index is compiled and managed by Solactive AG (the “Index Provider”).
The Manager (and each of its Connected Persons) is independent of the Index Provider.
Index Universe The index universe of the Index (“Index Universe”) is comprised of all financial instruments which fulfill the below requirements.

In respect of the Hong Kong Index Universe, it must be (i) listed on the SEHK; and (ii) one of the securities available via the Southbound Trading of the Shanghai -Hong Kong o r Shenzhen-Hong Kong Stock Connect Program on a Selection Day (as defined below under the section titled “Rebalance”) (the “Hong Kong Index Universe”).
In respect of the r emaining Index Universe, it must be listed on one of the International Stock Exchanges (the “Remaining Index Universe”).
Only one share class of each company is eligible for inclusion in the Index Universe.
If an eligible listing is available as part of both the Hong Kong Index Universe and the Remaining Index Universe, 195 only the Hong Kong listing will remain eligible.

If multiple listings are eligible within a single Index Universe region, the following buffer rules will apply to avoid frequent changes between share classes of a company: (i) If the company is currently included in the Index: The share class currently included in the Index will be eligible for the Index Universe if its minimum average daily value traded over 1 month and over 6 months prior to and including the Selection Day (“ Required Minimum ADV”) is at least 60% of the Required Minimum ADV of any other share class of the company.

If the Required Minimum ADV of the share class currently included in the Index falls below the 60% threshold, the relevant share class will be removed from the Index, and the share class with t he highest Required Minimum ADV will become eligible.
This determination will be based on data as of the Selection Day and implemented on the following Rebalance Day.
(ii) If the company is currently not included in the Index: The share class with the highest Required Minimum ADV will be included in the Index Universe.

Subject to the below, the securities must be classified as one of the following FactSet Revere Business Industry Classification System Focus (“ RBICS Focus ”, as further described below) industries: Other Gold Mining; Americas Gold Mining; Asia/Pacific Gold Mining; and Other Precious Metal Ore Mining.
Companies that are classified as Other Precious Metal Ore Mining must have the keyword “Gold” present in their FactSet business description to rema in eligible for Index inclusion.

As disclosed under the section headed “Selection Criteria” below, the Base Metal Mining industry group may form part of the Index Universe where there are less than 5 eligible securities from the Hong Kong Index Universe for inclusion in the Index.
The determination of the Index Universe is fully rule -based and the Index Provider cannot make any discretionary decisions.

RBICS Focus RBICS Focus offers a single-sector mapping of over 48,000 of the most liquid and publicly traded companies based on their primary lines of business; it uses revenues as the key factor in determining a company’s primary line of business.

Each company covered by the RBICS Focus system must derive a minimum of 50% of its revenue for the last finan cial year from a specific RBICS Level 5 industry to be classified as being focused on that industry.
The RBICS Focus system provides for 6 levels, from Level 1 (Economy) to Level 6 (Sub-industry), as follows: RBICS Level Name No.

of Groups 1 Economy 14 2 Sector 31 3 Sub-sector 89 4 Industry-Group 311 5 Industry 758 6 Sub-industry 1500 RBICS Focus industry descriptions Americas Gold Mining Companies engaged in gold mining and exploration in North America and Latin America Asia/Pacific Gold Mining Companies engaged in gold mining and exploration in Asia Other Gold Mining Companies engaged in gold mining and exploration in Europe, Africa, Russia, and globally Other Precious Metal Ore Mining Companies engaged in silver and platinum mining and exploration and in diversified precious metal mining 196 Selection Criteria Based on the Index Universe, the initial composition of the Index as well as any selection for an ordinary rebalance is determined on the Selection Day in accordance with the following rules.
The Index shall have 30 constituents at all times.
Eligible securities that are part of Hong Kong Index Universe are ranked based on their free float market capitalisation in a descending order.
In normal circumstances (i.e.

where there are 10 or more eligible securities from the Hong Kong Index Universe), the top 10 ranked securities by free float market capitalisation in the Hong Kong Index Universe are selected for Index inclusion.
The remaining 20 constituents are selecte d from the top -ranked securities in the Remaining Index Universe based on their free float market capitalisation in descending order.

 If there are less than 10 eligible securities from the Hong Kong Index Universe , all eligible securities that are part of the Remaining Index Universe are ranked based on their free float market capitalisation in a descending order and the top ranked securities are selected for Index inclusion until the Index contains 30 constituents.
 If there are less than 5 eligible secu rities from the Hong Kong Index Universe, additional securities belonging to the RBICS Focus industry group Base Metal Mining (i.e.

companies engaged in base metals mining and exploration, such as copper, nickel, and iron) that meet the Hong Kong Index Universe criteria will be included until 5 securities are selected for Index inclusion.
The remaining 25 constituents are then selected from the top-ranked securities in the Remaining Index Universe based on their free float market capitalisation in descending order.

Weighting On each Selection Day (as defined below), each component of the Index is assigned a weight according to free float market capitalisation with the following constraints: (i) The maximum weight of any single security shall not exceed 15%; (ii) The aggregate weight of securities that are part of the Hong Kong Index Universe will be fixed at 65% of the total Index weight; and (iii) The aggregate weight of Index components from the RBICS Focus industry groups Other Gold Mining; Americas Gold Mining; and Asia/Pacific Gold Mining will constitute at least 75% of the total Index weight.

In case additional securities belonging to the RBICS Focus industry group Base Metal Mining were selected, Index components from the RBICS Focus industry groups Other Gold Mining; Americas Gold Mining; Asia/Pacific Gold Mining; and Other Precious Metal Ore Mining are capped at a maximum of 15% per security, while Index components from the RBICS Focus industry group Base Metal Mining receive the residual weight required until the aggregate Hong Kong Index Universe constraint of 65% is met, and the aggregate weight of Index components from the RBICS Focus industry group Base Metal Mining is capped at 15% of the total Index weight.
Any excess weight is redistributed pro-rata among the Index components iteratively until all constraints are satisfied.

Rebalance Ordinary Rebalance In order to reflect the new selection of the Index constituents determined on the Selection Day (as defined below), the Index is adjusted on the Rebalance Day (as defined below) after close of 197 business.
“Selection Day” means 10 Business Days before the scheduled Rebalance Day, disregarding any potential change of the Rebalance Day.
“Rebalance Day” means the third Friday in March and September.
If that day is not a weekday or trading day, the Rebalance Day will be the immediately following trading day.
The Index Provider shall publish any changes made to the composition of the Index with sufficient notice before the Rebalance Day.
Extraordinary Rebalance The Index is not rebalanced extraordinarily.
Adjustments Under certain circumstances, an adjustment of the Index may be necessary between two regular Rebalance Days.
Such adjustment has to be made if a corporate action (as specified in the guideline of the Index) in relation of an Index component occurs.

Such adjustment may have to be done in relation to an Index constituent and/or may also affect the number of Index constituents and/or the weighting of certain Index constituents and will be made in compliance with the Solactive Equity Index Methodology, which is incorporated by reference and available on the website of the Index Provider: https://www.solactive.com/documents/equity-index-methodology/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).
The Index Provider will announce the Index adjustment giving a notice period of at least two Trading Days (with respect to the affected Index constituents) on the website of the Index Provider.
The Index adjustments will be implemented on the effective day specified in the respective notice.
Methodology Review The methodology of the Index is subject to regular review, at least annually.
In case a need of a change of the methodology has been identified within such review (e.g.
if the underlying market or economic reality has changed since the launch of the Index, i.e.

if the present methodology is based on obsolete assumptions and factors and no longer reflects the reality as accurately, reliably and appropriately as before), such change will be made in accordance with the Solactive Methodology Policy, which is incorporated by reference and available on the website of the Index Provider: https://www.solactive.com/documents/methodology-policy/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).

Such change in the methodology will be announced on the website of the Index Provider under the Section “News”, which is available at : https://www.solactive.com/news/announcements/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).

Index Committee An index committee composed of staff from the Index Provider and its subsidiaries (the “ Index Committee”) is responsible for decisions regarding any amendments to the rules of the Index.

Any such amendment, which may result in an amendment of the guideline of the Index, must be submitted to the Index Committee for prior approval and will be m ade in compliance with the methodology policy, which is available on the website of the Index Provider: https://www.solactive.com/documents/methodology-policy/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).

The list of the constituents of the Index, their respective weightings, additional information and other important news of the Index can be obtained from the website of the Index Provider at https://www.solactive.com/indices/?index=DE000SL0R4H1#detail (this website has not been 198 reviewed by the SFC).

Risks Factors Specific to the Sub-Fund In addition to the risk factors presented in Part 1 of this Prospectus, the risk factors set forth below are also the risks, in the opinion of the Manager and its directors, to be relevant and presently applicable specifically to the Sub-Fund.

Equity market risk The Sub-Fund’s investment in equity securities is subject to general market risks, whose value may fluctuate due to various factors, such as changes in investment sentiment, political and economic conditions and iss uer-specific factors.
New index risk The Index is a new index.
The Index has minimal operating history by which investors can evaluate its previous performance.
There can be no assurance as to the performance of the Index.
The Sub-Fund may be riskier than other exchange traded funds tracking more established indices with longer operating history.

Geographical concentration risk Due to the concentration of the Hong Kong, United States, Australia and Canada markets, the Index is subject to concentration risk and the performance of the Index may be more volatile when compared to other broad -based stock indices.

The Net Asset Value of the Sub -Fund is therefore likely to be more volatile than a more broad-based fund, such as a global fund, as the Index is more susceptible to fluctuations in value resulting from adverse conditions in Hong Kong, United States, Australia and Canada.

200 Political, economic and social risks in mainland China In tracking the Index, the Sub-Fund will invest in some companies the securities of which are listed on the SEHK and have substantial business operations in mainland China.
Such companies may have substantial exposure to the risks in mainland China.
As a result, changes in politic al, economic and social conditions in mainland China could adversely affect the value of investments.

Gold and precious metal ores mining sector concentration risk The constituents of the Index, and accordingly the Sub -Fund’s investments, may be concentrated in gold mining companies and other precious metal ore mining companies which have the keyword “Gold” present in their FactSet business description.

The value of the Sub -Fund may be more volatile than that of a fund having a more diverse portfolio of inv estments and may be more susceptible to adverse economic, political, policy, foreign exchange, liquidity, tax, legal or regulatory event affecting the relevant sector.
The price of gold may fluctuate significantly due to changes in global supply and demand , geopolitical events, and currency movements.

In addition, gold mining companies may face industry-specific risks such as operational challenges, regulatory changes, and environmental concerns, all of which can impact their profitability regardless of gold price movements.
There is no guarantee that the price of gold and other precious metal ores will appreciate.

The Sub- Fund may experience greater volatility and may be adversely affected by the performance of industries and sectors or events related to go ld and other precious metal ores and to their production and sale.
Risk associated with small-capitalisation / mid-capitalisation companies The Sub-Fund may invest in stocks of small-capitalisation / mid-capitalisation companies.

The stock of small-capitalisation / mid-capitalisation companies may have lower liquidity and their prices are more volatile to adverse economic developments than those of larger capitalisation companies in general.

Risk associated with investing in other collective investment schemes The Sub-Fund may invest in other collective investment schemes and will be subject to the risks associated with the underlying funds.

The Sub -Fund does not have control of the investments of the underlying funds and there is no assurance t hat the investment objective and strategy of the underlying funds will be successfully achieved which may have a negative impact to the Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund.
The underlying funds in which the Sub-Fund may invest may or may not be regulated by the SFC.
There may be additional costs involved when investing into these underlying funds.
There is also no guarantee that the underlying funds will always have sufficient liquidity to meet the Sub-Fund’s redemption requests as and when made.

Conflicts of interests may arise in a situation where the Sub-Fund invests in other funds managed by the Manager or its Connected Persons (despite that all initial charges and, where the underlying fund is managed by the Manager, all management fees and performance fee s on the underlying fund will be waived).
The Manager will use its best endeavours to avoid and resolve such conflicts fairly.

Risks associated with ADRs Exposure to American Depository Receipts (ADRs) may generate additional risks compared to a direct exposure to the corresponding underlying stocks, in particular, as the consequence of the intervention of the depositary bank issuing the ADR and the risk of non -segregation under applicable law of the depositary bank that holds the underlying stock as collateral and its own assets.

201 Although segregation is an integral part of the depositary agreement regulating the issuance of the ADRs, there could be a risk that underlying shares would not be attributed to holders of ADRs in case of bankruptcy of the depositary bank.
In such case, the likeliest scenario would be the trading suspension and thereafter a freeze of the price of the ADRs impacted by such bankruptcy event.
Bankruptcy events in respect of the depositary banks issuing the ADRs may negatively affect th e performance and/or the liquidity of the Sub-Fund.
There are fees related to ADRs, for example fees charged by banks for the custody of ADRs, which may impact the performance of the ADRs.
Also, holders of ADRs are not direct shareholders of the underlying company and generally do not have voting and other shareholder rights as shareholders do.
The Sub -Fund may also be subject to liquidity risk as ADRs are often less liquid than the corresponding underlying stocks.

Investment in FDIs risk The Sub-Fund may invest less than 30% of the Net Asset Value in FDIs (being futures and funded total return swaps) for investment and hedging purposes through one or more counterparty(ies).
As such, the Sub-Fund may suffer significant loss if a counterparty to the FDIs fai ls to perform its obligations, or in case of insolvency or default of the counterparty(ies).
Risks associated with FDIs include counterparty/credit risk, liquidity risk, valuation risk, volatility risk and over -the-counter transaction risk.
FDIs are susc eptible to price fluctuations and higher volatility, and may have large bid and offer spreads and no active secondary markets.
The leverage element/component of an FDI can result in a loss significantly greater than the amount invested in the FDI by the Sub-Fund.
Exposure to FDIs may lead to a high risk of significant loss by the Sub-Fund.

In the event that the Manager enters into funded total return swaps in respect of the Sub-Fund, the Manager will seek to mitigate the counterparty risks by fully collat eralising all counterparty exposures.
There is a risk that the value of the collateral may be substantially lower than the amount secured and so the Sub -Fund may suffer significant losses.
Any loss would result in a reduction in the Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund and impair the ability of the Sub-Fund to achieve its investment objective to track the Index.
The Sub -Fund may suffer significant losses if the counterparty fails to perform its obligations, defaults or becomes insolvent under the funded swaps.

The value of the collateral assets may be affected by market events and may diverge substantially from the performance of the Index, which may cause the Sub -Fund’s exposure to the swap counterparty to be under -collateralised and therefore result in significant losses.
Please refer to the risk factor headed “Financial derivative instruments risk” under “Risk Factors” in Part 1 of this Prospectus for further details.
The Manager has put in place measures to address the risks due to investment in FDIs.
For example, the Manager will ensure that counterparties to transactions of over -the-counter FDIs or their guarantors must be substantial financial institutions.

Please also refer to the sections “Financial Derivative Instruments ” and “Collateral” under “Investment Objective, Investment Strategy and Restrictions, Securities Lending and Borrowing ” in Part 1 of this Prospectus for a description of the Manager’s policy regarding FDIs and collateral.

Risks relating to securities lending transactions Collateral risk: As part of the securities lending transactions, the Sub -Fund must receive at least 100% of the valuation of the securities lent as collateral marked-to-market on a daily basis.
However, there is a risk of shortfall of collateral value due to inaccurate pricing of the collateral, adverse market movements in the collateral value, change of value of securities lent.
This may cause significant losses to the Sub -Fund if the borrower fails to return the securities lent out.
The Sub - Fund may also be subject to liquidity and custody risk of the collateral, as well as legal risk of 202 enforcement.
Counterparty risk: If the borrower defaults, there is a risk that the collateral held by the Sub-Fund may be realised at a value lower than the value of the securities lent.

This may be due to adverse market movements in the value of the collateral, intra -day increase in the value of the securities lent, a deterioration in the credit rating of the collateral issuer, default or insolvency of the collateral issuer or the illiquidity of the market in which the collateral is traded.
Operational risk: Securities lending transactions entail operational risks such as settlement failure or delays in the settlement of instructions.
There can be no assurance that the objective sought to be obtained from use of securities lending transactions will be achieved.

Trading differences risk As the underlying markets may be open when Units in the Sub -Fund are not priced, the value of the Securities in the Sub -Fund’s portfolio may change on days when investors will not be able to purchase or sell the Sub -Fund’s Units.

Furthermore, the market price of underlying Securities listed on the above stock exchanges which are established outside Hong Kong or swaps may not be available during part or all of the SEHK trading sessions due to trading hour differences which may result in the trading price of the Sub-Fund deviating away from the Net Asset Value.

Currency risk The Units traded in the RMB counter and the USD counter are denominated in a currency other than the Base Currency of the Sub -Fund and the Sub -Fund may also invest in securities denominated in a currency other than the base currency of the Sub-Fund.
The Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund may be affected unfavourably by fluctuations in the exchange rates between these currencies and the base currency and by changes in exchange rate controls.
RMB is currently not freely convertible and is subject to exchange controls and restrictions which, under exceptional circumstances, may cause a delay in payment of redemptions in RMB.
Although offshore RMB (CNH) and onshore RMB (CNY) are the same currency, they trade at different rates.
Any divergence between CNH and CNY may adversely impact investors.

Distribution out of/effectively out of capital risk Payment of dividends out of capital and/or effectively out of capital amounts to a return or withdrawal of part of an investor’s original investment or from any capital gains attributable to such original investments.
Any such distributions may result in an immediate reduction of the Net Asset Value per Unit of the Sub-Fund.
This may also reduce the capital that the Sub-Fund has available for investment in future and may constrain capital growth.

Multi-Counter risks There is a risk that the market price on the SEHK of Units traded in one counter may deviate significantly from the market price on the SEHK of Units traded in another counter due to different factors such as market liquidity, supply or demand in each counter and the exchange rate fluctuations.
The trading price of Units in each counter is determined by market forces and so will not be the same as the trading price of Units multiplied by the prevailing rate of foreign exchange.

Accordingly, when selling Units traded in one counter, an investor may receive less or pay more than the equivalent amount in the currency of another counter if the trade of the relevant Units took place on another counter.
There can be no assurance that the price of Units in each counter will be equivalent.
In June 2025, the HKEX has adopted a single ISIN approach for Multi -Counter eligible securities.

It is possible that some brokers and CCASS participants may not be familiar with this new model or may not be operationally ready, and as such may not be able to (i) buy Units in one counter and to sell Units in the other, or (ii) trade Units in both counters at the same time.
This may result in 203 potential settlement failure or delay.
In such a case another broker or CCASS participant may need to be used.

Accordingly, investors may only be able to trade their Units in one currency, investors are recommended to check the readiness of their brokers in respect of the Multi-Counter trading and inter-counter trading.

Reliance on Market Makers risk Although the Manager will ensure that at least one Market Maker will maintain a market for the Units traded in each counter, it should be noted that liquidity in the market for the Units may be adversely affected if there is no Market Maker for the HKD traded Units, the USD traded Units or the RMB traded Units.

The Manager will seek to mitigate this risk by ensuring at least one HKD Counter Market Maker, at least one USD Counter Market Maker and at least one RMB Counter Market Maker give not less than 3 months’ notice prior to terminating market making arrangement under the relevant market making agreements.
There may be less interest by potential market makers in making a market in RMB denominated or traded Units.
In particular, any disruption to the availability of RMB may adversely affect the capability of Market Makers in providing liquidity for such RMB traded Units.

It is possible that there will only be one SEHK Market Maker for each counter (HKD, USD or RMB) or the Manager may not be able to engage a substitute Market Maker within the termination notice period of a Market Maker.
There is also no guarantee that any market making activity will be effective.
Hong Kong Stamp Duty No stamp duty is payable in respect of any contract notes or ins truments of transfer relating to transactions in all Units (either HKD, USD or RMB traded Units).
No stamp duty is payable by an investor in relation to the creation of Units or the redemption of Units.

Investment Strategy Physical full replication strategy or representative sampling strategy.

Please refer to the section on “What is the Investment Strategy?” below Financial Year End 31 December Service Agent HK Conversion Agency Services Limited Website http://www.efunds.com.hk (the contents of this website have not been reviewed by the SFC) What is the Investment Objective?
The investment objective of the Sub-Fund is to provide investment results that, before deduction of fees and expenses, closely correspond to the performance of the Index.
There can be no assurance that the Sub-Fund will achieve its investment objective.
What is the Investment Strategy?

In seeking to achieve the Sub -Fund’s investment objective, the Manager will either use a full replication strategy or a representative sampling strategy as the Manager believes to be appropriate in order to achieve the investmen t objective of the Sub -Fund by tracking the Index as closely as possible to the benefit of the investors.
The Sub -Fund may switch between the full replication strategy and the representative sampling strategy in its absolute discretion without prior notice to investors.

In pursuing the full replication strategy, the Sub -Fund will invest in substantially all securities constituting the Index (the “ Index Securities”) which are common stocks securities listed on the SEHK, New York Stock Exchange, the Nasdaq S tock Exchange and the NYSE American Stock Exchange in substantially the same weightings (i.e.
proportions) as these Index Securities have in the Index.

In pursuing the representative sampling strategy, the Manager may: (i) invest in a representative sample whose performance is closely correlated with the Index, but whose constituents may or may not themselves be constituents of the Index; (ii) invest less than 30% of the Sub -Fund’s Net Asset Value in collective investment schemes (“CIS”), each being either an exch ange traded fund or an unlisted index tracking fund which tracks an index that has a high correlation with the Index.
Such CIS may be authorised by the SFC, eligible schemes under Chapter 7.11A of the Code, or non-eligible schemes.

For the avoidance of dou bt, the Sub -Fund’s aggregate investment in non-eligible schemes and not authorised by the SFC may not exceed 10% of the Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund; and/or (iii) invest in financial derivative instruments (“FDIs”) including futures and swaps with less than 30% of the Sub -Fund’s Net Asset Value for investment and hedging purposes, where the Manager believes such investments will help the Sub -Fund achieve its investment objective and are beneficial to the Sub -Fund.

The swaps which may be invested by the Sub -Fund will be funded total return swap transaction(s) whereby the Sub-Fund will pass on the relevant portion of cash to the swap counterparty(ies) and in return the swap counterparty(ies) will provide the Sub -Fund with an exposure to the 206 economic gain/loss in the performance of the relevant securities (net of indirect costs).
The Manager is independent of the swap counterparties.

Each swap counterparty for such transactions will be independent counterparties approved by the Manager with credit rating of at least BBB- (by Moody’s or Standard & Poor’s, or any other equivalent ratings by recognised credit rating agencies) or which are corporations licensed by the SFC or are registered institutions with the Hong Kong Monetary Authority or substantial financial institutions.
The swap counterparties are monitored on an on -going basis as well as reviewed at least once a year by the Manager to ensure they continue to meet the aforesaid selection criteria.

In pursuing a representative sampling strategy, the Manager may cause the Sub-Fund to deviate from the Index weighting on condition that the maximum deviation from the Index weighting of any constituent will not exceed 3% or such other percentage as determined by the Manager after consultation with the SFC.

Other investments The Sub-Fund may invest in money market funds (subject to the investment restrictions as set out in Chapter 7 of the Code), and in cash deposits and cash equivalents for cash management purposes, up to 5% of its Net Asset Value.

The Manager may, on behalf of the Sub -Fund, enter into securities lending transactions, with the maximum level for up to 50% and expected level for approximately 20% of its Net Asset Value, and is able to recall the securities lent out at any time.
All securities len ding transactions will only be carried out in the best interest of the Sub -Fund and as set out in the relevant securities lending agreement.
Such transactions may be terminated at any time by the Manager at its absolute discretion.

As part of the securitie s lending transactions, the Sub -Fund must receive cash and/or non -cash collateral of at least 100% of the value of the securities lent (interests, dividends and other eventual rights included).
The collateral will be marked -to-market on a daily basis and b e safekept by the Custodian.
The valuation of the collateral generally takes place on trading day T.

If the value of the collateral falls below 100% of the value of the securities lent on any trading day T, the Manager will call for additional collateral o n trading day T, and the borrower will have to deliver additional collateral to make up for the difference in securities value, with settlement of such delivery expected to occur on or before trading day T+2.
Non-cash collateral received may not be sold, re-invested or pledged.

Please refer to “Collateral” under the section headed “Investment Objective, Strategy and Restrictions” in Part 1 of this Explanatory Memorandum for details of the requirements in relation to collateral received as part of the securities lending transactions.

In conducting securities lending transactions, the Manager will select independent counterparties approved by the Manager which (i) are incorporated in countries of high credit quality; (ii) have a minimum long-term credit rating of A2 or short -term credit rating of P2 by Moody’s or equivalent assigned by reputable credit rating agencies; or (iii) be a licensed corporation with the SFC or registered institution with the Hong Kong Monetary Authority.

All the revenues arising from securities lending transactions, net of direct and indirect expenses as reasonable and normal compensation for the services rendered in the context of the securities financing transactions to the extent permitted by applicable legal and regulatory requirements, shall be returned to the Sub-Fund.

Please refer to “Securities financing transactions” under the section headed “Investment Objective, Strategy and Restrictions” in Part 1 of this Explanatory Memorandum for details of the Manager’s policy in relation to securities lending transactions.
The Manager does not currently intend to enter into sale and repurchase transactions, reverse repurchase transactions and other similar over-the-counter transactions on behalf of the Sub-Fund.

The Manager will seek prior approval of the SFC (to the extent required under applicable regulatory requirements) and provide at least one month’s prior notice to Unitholders before the Manager 207 engages in any such transactions.
The investment strategy of the Sub-Fund is subject to the investment and borrowing restrictions set out in Part 1 of this Prospectus.

The Index This section is a brief overview of the Index.
It contains a summary of the principal features of the Index and is not a complete description o f the Index.
As at the date of this Prospectus, the summary of the Index in this section is accurate and consistent with the complete description of the Index.
Complete information on the Index appears in the website identified below.
Such information may change from time to time and details of the changes will appear on that website.

General Information on the Index The Index is Wind Digital Technology Net T otal Return Index, which is a net total return, adjusted capitalisation weighted index that mainly tracks the overall performance of the companies listed in Hong Kong and the United States with digital technology t hemes.
Constituent companies may include companies listed on the SEHK and companies that are listed on the New York Stock Exchange, the Nasdaq Stock Exchange and the NYSE American Stock Exchange.

Constituents of the Index are securities of companies from the following themes: digital technology and service providers, digital infrastructure validation technology, digital asset transactional technology, and enterprises utilising digital technology for treasury management (collectively, the “Digital Technology Themes”).
The Index is a net total return index which means that its performance reflects the reinvestment of dividends and distributions, net of withholding tax, from the Index constituents.
The Index was launched on 25 September 2025 and had a base level of 1000 on 18 September 2020.
As of 31 December 2025, the Index had a total market capitalisation of HKD13,626.5 billion and 30 constituents.
Index Provider The Index is compiled and managed by Wind Information Technology Co., Ltd (the “ Index Provider”).
The Manager (and each of its Connected Persons) is independent of the Index Provider.

Index Universe The index universe of the Index (“Index Universe”) is comprised of all financial instruments which fulfill the below requirements: (i) Hong Kong: All companies listed on the SEHK that are eligible for the Stock Connect program (the “Hong Kong Index Universe”).
(ii) United States: All companies listed on the New York Stock Exchange, the Nasdaq 214 Stock Exchange and the NYSE American Stock Exchange (the “US Index Universe”).
Selection method Components of the Index are selected from the Index Universe based on the following selection method: (i) Companies must have been listed for at least 30 days.
(ii) The average daily trading volume in the past three months is more than HKD 3 million; the average daily turnover rate in the past year ranks in the top 80% of securities in its market.
In case the companies have not been listed for over three months, the average daily trading volume during the period of which the Companies has been listed would be considered.
In case the companies have not been listed for over one year, the average daily turnover rate during the period of which the Companies has been listed will be considered.

(iii) Industry mapping: Companies will be identified by Wind Global Industry Classification Standard (WGICS)11 with business segments that are most directly related and relevant to the Digital T echnology Themes as follows: (a) Information Technology — Technology Hardware & Equipment — Technology Hardware, Storage & Peripherals (b) Information T echnology — T echnology Hardware & Equipment — Electronic Equipment, Instruments & Components (c) Information Technology — Software & Services — IT Services (d) Information Technology — Software & Services — Software (e) Financials — Financial Services & Insurance — Financial Services (f) Financials — Financial Services & Insurance — Capital Markets (g) Industrials — Commercial & Professional Services — Professional Services Systematic term-based screening: Screening process is conducted in parallel through reviewing the company profile, product information, and public announcements, as well as research reports published by securities research institutions in relation to these companies to identify the following key terms to confirm that its actual business operations and segments are relevant to the Digital Technology Themes: (a) Digital Technology (b) Cloud Computing (c) Fintech 11 The Wind Global Industry Classification Standard is used as the classification basis, which was launched by Wind Information Technology Co., Ltd in 2004 to meet market investment research needs, and has become one of the mainstream industry classific ation standards in the financial market.
The key basis used for the Wind Global Industry Classification Standard is source of main business income.

The Wind Global Industry Classification Standard document is available at https://www.windindices.com/indicesWebsite/api/preview?file=Wind%E5%85%A8%E7%90%83%E8%A1%8C%E4%B8%9A%E5%88 %86%E7%B1%BB%E6%A0%87%E5%87%86%EF%BC%882024%EF%BC% 89.pdf (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC) on the Index Provider’s website at https://www.windindices.com/indices/zh/AboutUs/WindIndexIntroduction (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).
215 (d) Blockchain (e) Digital Assets The list of key terms is subject to review on a regular basis.

(iv) Each company selected after the above step will be tested through a scoring system and given a thematic correlation coefficient based on the Wind Thematic Correlation Coefficient Scoring Rules (see the response below).
Samp les with a correlation coefficient of less than 0.1 will be excluded from the sample space for the Index.
If the sample is insufficient, it shall be supplemented with stocks from the corresponding market's information technology sector.
Please refer to the rules below for further information.
Thematic correlation coefficient The thematic correlation coefficient is an indicator that measures the degree of association between a listed company and that specific investment theme.
The Wind Thematic Correlation Coefficient Scoring Rules are as follows.
(1) Scoring is conducted across three dimensions: Main Business, T echnology, and Dynamics.
The scoring coefficient ranges between 0 and 1.
The weighting is distributed as follows: Main Business (50%), T echnology (30%), and Dynamics (20%).

(2) Main Business scoring: Based on the business revenue data by industry and product disclosed by the company, the thematic correlation coefficient is determined by multiplying the proportion of theme-related revenue to total operating revenue by the scoring coefficient.
 Main Business Score = Revenue Share of Thematic Related Business * Thematic Business Scoring Coefficient.

 Example: For a New Energy Vehicle (NEV) thematic index evaluation, a battery manufacturer has 57% of its revenue from lithium batteries applied to NEVs and 43% from lithium batteries applied to consumer electronics.
The scoring coefficient for the NEV lithium batt ery business is 1, and for the consumer electronics lithium battery business is 0.
When compiling the NEV thematic index, the Main Business Score for this enterprise = 57% * 1 = 0.57.
(3) Technology scoring: The company will be assessed whether it possesses co re technologies corresponding to the theme.
Reference bases include:  Textual descriptions in listed company disclosures such as company announcements and annual reports;  Textual descriptions in institutional research reports.
(4) Dynamics Scoring: The latest dynamics of the company will be assessed such as market feedback and report coverage.
(5) A weighted comprehensive score (rounded to 1 decimal place) will be calculated to obtain the thematic correlation coefficient.

(v) The Index samples 30 companies in total, with 10 companies selected from the Hong Kong Index Universe and 20 companies selected from the US Index Universe, sorted by the total market capitalization adjusted by the thematic correlation coefficient calculated as follows: 216 Total Market Capitalization * Thematic Correlation Coefficient.

Insufficient sample If the sample in step (v) above is less than 30 companies, the sample shall be supplemented with stocks from the corresponding market's information technology sector based on the rules described below.
(1) Select stocks from the Information T echnology industry in the Wind Global Industry Classification to form the initial screening stock pool.

Reason for selecting Information Technology when adding samples: In the index construction process , the Wind Global Industry Classification is used to determine the thematic industry candidate sample space.
According to the correspondence table between Wind Global Industries and the Index Definition Scope, the Information Technology industry has a very high degree of correlation with the Index theme.
The vast majority of constituent samples related to "Digital technology and service providers" and "Digital infrastructure validation technology" originate from the Information Technology industry.
Therefor e, to ensure the integrity of the index theme, components from Information Technology are prioritized for adding samples.
(2) Perform data processing such as listing time and liquidity screening according to the methodology.
(3) Conduct thematic correlation coefficient assessment according to the Wind Thematic Correlation Coefficient Scoring Rules.
(4) Sort samples in descending order based on the thematic correlation coefficient score, and select the top-ranked samples sequentially.

(5) Added Sample Weighting Rule s: Since added samples generally have lower thematic correlation coefficients, to maintain the high correlation characteristic of the Index theme, the weight cap for each added sample is set at 0.1%.
Rebalancing The rebalancing of the Index occurs on the trading day following the third Friday of March, June, September and December each year.
Weighting rules Weights are assigned based on adjusted market capitalization (i.e.

total issued and listed shares including shares subject to lock -up provisions), with a distribution of 65% for Hong Kong -listed components that are eligible for the Stock Connect program and 35% for United States -listed components.
The maximum weight for a single component is 15%.
Index Maintenance When a component stock of the Index is delisted, it is removed from the Index on the same day.

(Hong Kong time) quoted by Bloomberg as of the same Dealing Day.

Risks Factors Specific to the Sub-Fund In addition to the risk factors presented in Part 1 of this Prospectus, the risk factors set forth below are also the risks, in the opinion of the Manager and its directors, to be relevant and presently applicable specifically to the Sub-Fund.

Equity market risk The Sub-Fund’s investment in equity securities is sub ject to general market risks, whose value may fluctuate due to various factors, such as changes in investment sentiment, political and economic conditions and issuer -specific factors.
New index risk The Index is a new index.
The Index has minimal operating history by which investors can evaluate its previous performance.
There can be no assurance as to the performance of the Index.
The Sub-Fund may be riskier than other exchange traded funds tracking more established indices with longer operating h istory.

Geographical concentration risk Due to the concentration of the Hong Kong and the United States markets, the Index is subject to concentration risk and the performance of the Index may be more volatile when compared to other broad-based stock indic es.

The Net Asset Value of the Sub -Fund is therefore likely to be more volatile than a more broad-based fund, such as a global fund, as the Index is more susceptible to fluctuations in value resulting from adverse conditions in Hong Kong and the United States.

Political, economic and social risks in mainland China 219 In tracking the Index, the Sub-Fund will invest in some companies the securities of which are listed on the SEHK and have substantial business operations in mainland China.
Such companies may have substantial exposure to the risks in mainland China.
As a result, changes in political, economic and social conditions in mainland China could adversely affect the value of investments.

Digital technology themes concentration risk The constituents of the Index, and accordingly the Sub-Fund’s investments, may be concentrated in companies operating within digital technology and service provision, digital infrastructure validation, digital asset transactional technology, and enterprises utilising digital technology for treasury management.

The value of the Sub-Fund may be more volatile than that of a fund having a more diverse portfolio of investments and may be more susceptible to adverse economic, political, policy, foreign exchange, liquidity, tax, legal or regulatory event affecting the relevant sector.

The digital technology theme of the Index, hence the underlying securities, encompasses areas such as digital infrastructure validation, digital asset transaction platforms, and technology -driven treasury management solutions.
This presents risks such as high computational demands and cybersecurity vulnerabilities, as well as regulatory challenges such as compliance with evolving laws and intellectual property disputes.
The business for companies in this theme is subject to high volatility which may in turn have adverse effects on the Sub-Fund.
Many of the companies with a high business exposure to a digital technology theme have a relatively short operating history.
Digital technology companies are often characterised by relatively higher volatility in price performance when compared to other economic sectors.
Companies in the digital technology sector also face intense competition, and there may also be substantial government intervention, which may have an adverse effect on profit margins.
Rapid changes could render obsolete the products and services offered by these companies.

These companies are also subject to the risks of loss or impairment of intellectual property rights or licences, cyber security risks resulting in undesirable legal, financial, operational and reputational consequences.
A downturn in the business for companies in the digital asset sector may have adverse effects on the Sub-Fund.
Risk associated with small-capitalisation / mid-capitalisation companies The Sub-Fund may invest in stocks of small-capitalisation / mid-capitalisation companies.

The stock of small-capitalisation / mid-capitalisation companies may have lower liquidity and their prices are more volatile to adverse economic developments than those of larger capitalisation companies in general.

Risk associated with investing in other collective investment schemes The Sub-Fund may invest in other collective investment schemes and will be subject to the risks associated with the underlying funds.

The Sub -Fund does not have control of the investments of the underlying funds and there is no assurance that the investment objective and strategy of the underlying funds will be successfully achieved which may have a negative impact to the Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund.
The underlying funds in which the Sub-Fund may invest may or may not be regulated by the SFC.
There may be additional costs involved when investing into these underlying funds.
There is also no guarantee that the underlying funds will always have sufficient liquidity to meet the Sub-Fund’s redemption requests as and when made.

Conflicts of interests may arise in a situation where the Sub-Fund invests in other funds managed by the Manager or its Connected Persons (despite that all initial charges and, where the underlying fund is managed by the Manager, all management fees and performance fees on the underlying fund will be waived).
The Manager will use its best endeavours to avoid and resolve such conflicts fairly.

220 Risks associated with ADRs Exposure to American Depository Receipts (ADRs) may generate additional risks compared to a direct exposure to the corresponding underlying stocks, in particular, as the consequence of the intervention of the depositary bank issu ing the ADR and the risk of non -segregation under applicable law of the depositary bank that holds the underlying stock as collateral and its own assets.

Although segregation is an integral part of the depositary agreement regulating the issuance of the ADRs, there could be a risk that underlying shares would not be attributed to holders of ADRs in case of bankruptcy of the depositary bank.
In such case, the likeliest scenario would be the trading suspension and thereafter a freeze of the price of the ADRs impacted by such bankruptcy event.
Bankruptcy events in respect of the depositary banks issuing the ADRs may negatively affect the performance and/or the liquidity of the Sub-Fund.
There are fees related to ADRs, for example fees charged by banks for the custody of ADRs, which may impact the performance of the ADRs.
Also, holders of ADRs are not direct shareholders of the underlying company and generally do not have voting and other shareholder rights as shareholders do.
The Sub -Fund may also be subject to liquidity risk as ADRs are often less liquid than the corresponding underlying stocks.

Investment in FDIs risk The Sub-Fund may invest less than 30% of the Net Asset Value in FDIs (being futures and funded total return swaps) for investment and hedging pur poses through one or more counterparty(ies).
As such, the Sub-Fund may suffer significant loss if a counterparty to the FDIs fails to perform its obligations, or in case of insolvency or default of the counterparty(ies).
Risks associated with FDIs include counterparty/credit risk, liquidity risk, valuation risk, volatility risk and over -the-counter transaction risk.
FDIs are susceptible to price fluctuations and higher volatility, and may have large bid and offer spreads and no active secondary markets.
The leverage element/component of an FDI can result in a loss significantly greater than the amount invested in the FDI by the Sub-Fund.
Exposure to FDIs may lead to a high risk of significant loss by the Sub-Fund.

In the event that the Manager enters into funded total return swaps in respect of the Sub-Fund, the Manager will seek to mitigate the counterparty risks by fully collateralising all counterparty exposures.
There is a risk that the value of the collateral may be substantially lower than the amount secured and so the Sub -Fund may suffer significant losses.
Any loss would result in a reduction in the Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund and impair the ability of the Sub-Fund to achieve its investment objective to track the Index.
The Sub -Fund may suff er significant losses if the counterparty fails to perform its obligations, defaults or becomes insolvent under the funded swaps.

The value of the collateral assets may be affected by market events and may diverge substantially from the performance of the Index, which may cause the Sub -Fund’s exposure to the swap counterparty to be under -collateralised and therefore result in significant losses.
Please refer to the risk factor headed “Financial derivative instruments risk” under “Risk Factors” in Part 1 of this Prospectus for further details.
The Manager has put in place measures to address the risks due to investment in FDIs.
For example, the Manager will ensure that counterparties to transactions of over -the-counter FDIs or their guarantors must be sub stantial financial institutions.

Please also refer to the sections “Financial Derivative Instruments ” and “Collateral” under “Investment Objective, Investment Strategy and Restrictions, Securities Lending and Borrowing ” in Part 1 of this Prospectus for a description of the Manager’s policy regarding FDIs and collateral.

Risks relating to securities lending transactions Collateral risk: As part of the securities lending transactions, the Sub -Fund must receive at least 100% of the valuation of the securities lent as collateral marked-to-market on a daily basis.
However, 221 there is a risk of shortfall of collateral value due to inaccurate pricing of the collateral, adverse market movements in the collateral value, change of value of securities lent.
This may cau se significant losses to the Sub -Fund if the borrower fails to return the securities lent out.
The Sub - Fund may also be subject to liquidity and custody risk of the collateral, as well as legal risk of enforcement.
Counterparty risk: If the borrower defaults, there is a risk that the collateral held by the Sub -Fund may be realised at a value lower than the value of the securities lent.

This may be due to adverse market movements in the value of the collateral, intra -day increase in the value of the securiti es lent, a deterioration in the credit rating of the collateral issuer, default or insolvency of the collateral issuer or the illiquidity of the market in which the collateral is traded.
Operational risk: Securities lending transactions entail operational risks such as settlement failure or delays in the settlement of instructions.
There can be no assurance that the objective sought to be obtained from use of securities lending transactions will be achieved.

Trading differences risk As the underlying markets may be open when Units in the Sub -Fund are not priced, the value of the Securities in the Sub -Fund’s portfolio may change on days when investors will not be able to purchase or sell the Sub -Fund’s Units.

Furthermore, the market price of underlying Secur ities listed on the above stock exchanges which are established outside Hong Kong or swaps may not be available during part or all of the SEHK trading sessions due to trading hour differences which may result in the trading price of the Sub-Fund deviating away from the Net Asset Value.

Currency risk The Units traded in the RMB counter and the USD counter are denominated in a currency other than the Base Currency of the Sub -Fund and the Sub -Fund may also invest in securities denominated in a currency other than the Base Currency of the Sub-Fund.
The Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund may be affected unfavourably by fluctuations in the exchange rates between these currencies and the base currency and by changes in exchange rate controls.
RMB is currently not freely convertible and is subject to exchange controls and restrictions which, under exceptional circumstances, may cause a delay in payment of redemptions in RMB.
Although offshore RMB (CNH) and onshore RMB (CNY) are the same currency, they trade at different rates.
Any divergence between CNH and CNY may adversely impact investors.

Distribution out of/effectively out of capital risk Payment of dividends out of capital and/or effectively out of capital amounts to a return or withdrawal of part of an investor’s original investment or from any capital gains attributable to such original investments.
Any such distributions may result in an immediate reduction of the Net Asset Value per Unit of the Sub-Fund.
This may also reduce the capital that the Sub-Fund has available for investment in future and may constrain capital growth.

Multi-Counter risks There is a risk that the market price on the SEHK of Units traded in one counter may deviate significantly from the market price on the SEHK of Units traded in another counter due to different factors such as market liquidity, supply or demand in each counter and the exchange rate fluctuations.
The trading price of Units in each counter is determined by market forces and so will not be the same as the trading price of Units multiplied by the prevailing rate of foreign exchange.

Accordingly, when selling Units traded in one counter, an investor may receive less or pay more than the equivalent amount in the currency of another counter if the trade of the relevant Units took place on another counter.
There can be no assurance that the price of Units in each counter will be equivalent.
222 In June 2025, the HKEX has adopted a single ISIN approach for Multi -Counter eligible securities.

It is possible that some brokers and CCASS participants may not be familiar with this new model or may not be operationally ready, and as such may not be able to (i) buy Units in one counter and to sell Units in the other, or (ii) tra de Units in both counters at the same time.
This may result in potential settlement failure or delay.
In such a case another broker or CCASS participant may need to be used.

Accordingly, investors may only be able to trade their Units in one currency, investors are recommended to check the readiness of their brokers in respect of the Multi-Counter trading and inter-counter trading.

Reliance on Market Makers risk Although the Manager will ensure that at least one Market Maker will maintain a market for the Units traded in each counter, it should be noted that liquidity in the market for the Units may be adversely affected if there is no Market Maker for the HKD traded Units, the USD traded Units or the RMB traded Units.

The Manager will seek to mitigate thi s risk by ensuring at least one HKD Counter Market Maker, at least one USD Counter Market Maker and at least one RMB Counter Market Maker give not less than 3 months’ notice prior to terminating market making arrangement under the relevant market making agreements.
There may be less interest by potential market makers in making a market in RMB denominated or traded Units.
In particular, any disruption to the availability of RMB may adversely affect the capability of Market Makers in providing liquidity f or such RMB traded Units.

It is possible that there will only be one SEHK Market Maker for each counter (HKD, USD or RMB) or the Manager may not be able to engage a substitute Market Maker within the termination notice period of a Market Maker.
There is also no guarantee that any market making activity will be effective.
Hong Kong Stamp Duty No stamp duty is payable in respect of any contract notes or instruments of transfer relating to transactions in all Units (either HKD, USD or RMB traded Units).
No stamp duty is payable by an investor in relation to the creation of Units or the redemption of Units.

Financial Year End 31 December Service Agent HK Conversion Agency Services Limited Website http://www.efunds.com.hk (the contents of this website have not been reviewed by the SFC) What is the Investment Objective?
The investment objective of the Sub-Fund is to provide investment results that, before deduction of fees and expenses, closely correspond to the performance of the Index.
There can be no assurance that the Sub-Fund will achieve its investment objective.
What is the Investment Strategy?

In seeking to achieve the Sub -Fund’s investment objective, the Manager will either use a full replication strategy or a representative sampling strategy as the Manager believes to be appropriate in order to achieve the investment objective of the Sub -Fund by tracking the Index as closely as possible to the benefit of the investors.
The Sub -Fund may switch between the full replication strategy and the representative sampling strategy in its absolute discretion without prior notice to investors.

In pursuing t he full replication strategy, the Sub -Fund will invest in substantially all securities constituting the Index (the “ Index Securities”) which are common stocks securities listed on a regulated stock exchange in Hong Kong and the United States (including Ame rican Depositary Receipts (ADRs) in substantially the same weightings (i.e.
proportions) as these Index Securities have in the Index.

In pursuing the representative sampling strategy, the Manager may: (i) invest in a representative sample whose performance is closely correlated with the Index, but whose constituents may or may not themselves be constituents of the Index; (ii) invest less than 30% of the Sub -Fund’s Net Asset Value in collective investment schemes (“CIS”), each being either an exchange traded fund o r an unlisted index tracking fund which tracks an index that has a high correlation with the Index.
Such CIS may be authorised by the SFC, eligible schemes under Chapter 7.11A of the Code, or non-eligible schemes.

For the avoidance of doubt, the Sub -Fund’s aggregate investment in non-eligible schemes and not authorised by the SFC may not exceed 10% of the Net Asset Value of the Sub-Fund; and/or (iii) invest in financial derivative instruments (“FDIs”) including futures and swaps with less than 30% of the Sub-Fund’s Net Asset Value for investment and hedging purposes, where the Manager believes such investments will help the Sub -Fund achieve its investment objective and are beneficial to the Sub -Fund.

The swaps which may be invested by the Sub -Fund will be funded total return swap transaction(s) whereby the Sub-Fund will pass on the relevant portion of cash to the swap counterparty(ies) and in return the swap counterparty(ies) will provide the Sub -Fund with an exposure to the economic gain/loss in the performance of the relevant securities (net of indirect costs).
The Manager is independent of the swap counterparties.

Each swap counterparty for such transactions will be independent counterparties approved by the Manager with credit rating of at least BBB- (by Moody’s or Standard & Poor’s, or any other equivalent ratings by recognised credit rating agencies) or which are corporations licensed by the SFC or are registered institutions with the Hong Kong Monetary Authority or substantial 225 financial institutions.
The swap counterparties are monitored on an on-going basis as well as reviewed at least once a year by the Manager to ensure they continue to meet the aforesaid selection criteria.

In pursuing a representative sampling strategy, the Manager may cause the Sub-Fund to deviate from the Index weighting on condition that the maximum deviation from the Index weighting of any constituent will not exceed 3% or such other percentage as determined by the Manager after consultation with the SFC.

Other investments The Sub-Fund may invest in money market funds (subject to the investment restrictions as set out in Chapter 7 of the Code), and in cash deposits and cash equivalents for cash management purposes, up to 5% of its Net Asset Value.

The Manager may, on behalf of the Sub-Fund, enter into securities lending transactions, with the maximum level for up to 50% and expected level for approximately 20% of its Net Asset Value, and is able to recall the securities lent out at any time.
All securities lending transactions will only be carried out in the best interest of the Sub -Fund and as set out in the relevant securities lending agreement.
Such transactions may be terminated at any time by the Manager at its absolute discretion.

As part of the securities lending transactions, the Sub -Fund must receive cash and/or non -cash collateral of at least 100% of the value of the securities lent (interests, dividends and other eventual rights included).
The collateral will be marked -to-market on a daily basis and be safekept by the Custodian.
The valuation of the collateral generally takes place on trading day T.

If the value of the collateral falls below 100% of the value of the securities lent on any trading day T, the Manager will call for additional collateral on trading day T, and the borrower will have to deliver additional collateral to make up for the difference in securities value, with settlement of such delivery expected to occur on or before trading day T+2.
Non-cash collateral received may not be sold, re-invested or pledged.

Please refer to “Collateral” under the section headed “Investment Objective, Strategy and Restrictions” in Part 1 of this Explanatory Memorandum for details of the requirements in relation to collateral received as part of the securities lending transactions.

In conducting securities lending transactions, the Manager will select independent counterparties approved by the Manager which (i) are incorporated in countries of high credit quality; (ii) have a minimum long-term credit rating of A2 or short -term credit rating of P2 by Moody’s or equivalent assigned by reputable credit rating agencies; or (iii) be a licensed corporation with the SFC or registered institution with the Hong Kong Monetary Authority.

All th e revenues arising from securities lending transactions, net of direct and indirect expenses as reasonable and normal compensation for the services rendered in the context of the securities financing transactions to the extent permitted by applicable legal and regulatory requirements, shall be returned to the Sub-Fund.

Please refer to “Securities financing transactions” under the section headed “Investment Objective, Strategy and Restrictions” in Part 1 of this Explanatory Memorandum for details of the Mana ger’s policy in relation to securities lending transactions.
The Manager does not currently intend to enter into sale and repurchase transactions, reverse repurchase transactions and other similar over-the-counter transactions on behalf of the Sub-Fund.

The Manager will seek prior approval of the SFC (to the extent required under applicable regulatory requirements) and provide at least one month’s prior notice to Unitholders before the Manager engages in any such transactions.
The investment strategy of the Sub-Fund is subject to the investment and borrowing restrictions set out in Part 1 of this Prospectus.

General Information on the Index The Solactive Biopharma Select Index (the “ Index”) is designed to represent the performance of leading companies engaged in biopharmaceuticals in Hong Kong and the United States of America.

All constituents selected must be listed on at least one of the following stock exchanges: the Hong Kong Stock Exchange (SEHK), New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), NASDAQ, or NYSE American, including all exchange traded American Depositary Receipts (ADRs).
The Index is a net total return index, i.e.
the performance of the Index reflects the reinvestment of dividends and distributions, net of withholding tax, from the Index constituents .
The Index is denominated and quoted in HKD.
The Index was launched on 30 September 2025 and had a base level of 1,000 on 20 March 2020.
As of 6 January 2026, the Index had a total market capitalisation of approximately HKD 38.60 trillion and 96 constituents.
Index Provider The Index is compiled and managed by Solactive AG (the “Index Provider” or “Solactive”).
The Manager (and each of its Connected Persons) is independent of the Index Provider.
Index Universe 232 The index universe of the Index (“Index Universe”) is comprised of all financial instruments which fulfill the below requirements.
For the Hong Kong Index Universe: - Must be listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange.
- Only securities available via the Southbound Trading of the Shanghai -Hong Kong or Shenzhen-Hong Kong Stock Connect Program on a Selection Day are eligible for inclusion.
For the United States Index Universe , the s ecurities m ust be listed on one of the following exchanges: New York Stock Exchange, NASDAQ, or NYSE America n, and all exchange traded ADRs.

General requirements: - Minimum average daily value traded of at least USD3,000,000 over 6 months for securities that are not Index components and at least USD2,000,000 for companies that are Index components prior to and including the Selection Day.
Companies which are listed less than 6 months are included if their average daily value traded over the period since they are listed is at least USD 3,000,000.
- Only one share class of each company is eligible for inclusion in the Index Universe.
If an eligible listing is available as part of both the Hong Kong and United States Index Universe, only the Hong Kong listing wi ll remain eligible.

Buffer rules apply to avoid frequent changes between share classes : - If the company is currently included in the Index: The share class currently included in the Index will be eligible for the Index Universe if its minimum average daily value traded over 1 month and over 6 months prior to and including the Selection Day is at least 60% of the minimum average daily value traded over 1 month and over 6 months prior to and including the Selection Day of any other share class of the company.

If the share class currently included in the Index falls below the 60% threshold, it will be removed from the Index, and the share class with the highest minimum average daily value traded over 1 month and over 6 months prior to and including the Selection Day will become eligible.
This determination will be based on data as of the Selection Day and implemented on the following Rebalance Day.

- If the company is currently not included in the Index: The share class with the highest minimum average daily value traded over 1 month and over 6 months prior to and including the Selection Day is included in the Index Universe.
“Selection Day ” is 10 calculation days before the scheduled Rebalance Day, disregarding any potential change of the Rebalance Day.
“Rebalance Day” is the third Friday in March and September.
If that day is not a calculation day or trading day the rebalance day will be the immediately following trading day.
RBICS Classification The securities m ust be classified under the FactSet Revere Business Industry Classification System (“RBICS”) as the “Biopharmaceuticals ” sector (RBICS Sector Number 3510).

Selection of Index Components 233 Based on the Index Universe, the initial composition of the Index as well as any selection for an ordinary rebalance is determined on the Selection Day in accordance with the following rules: The Index shall have 100 constituents, consisting of up to 30 securities from the Hong Kong Index Universe and at least 70 securities from the United States Index Universe.
Eligible securities that are part of the Hong Kong Index Universe are ranked based on their total market capitalisation i n descending order.
The top 30 ranked securities are eligible for Index inclusion.
In case less than 30 securities are eligible, all eligible Hong Kong securities are selected for Index inclusion.
Eligible securities that are part of the United States Ind ex Universe are ranked based on their total market capitalisation in descending order.
The top ranked securities are eligible for Index inclusion until the Index contains 100 securities.

Weighting On each Selection Day, each component of the Index is ass igned a weight according to the following rules: - The aggregate weight of Index components that are part of the Hong Kong Index Universe will be fixed at 65% of the total Index weight.
All Hong Kong Index components are equally weighted.
- The aggregate weight of Index components that are part of the United States Index Universe will be fixed at 35% of the total Index weight.
All United States Index components are equally weighted.
Rebalance Ordinary Rebalance The Index is rebalanced semi -annually, on the third Friday in March and September.
The Index is adjusted after close of business on the Rebalance Day, based on the composition and weights determined on the Selection Day (10 calculation days before the Rebalance Day).
The Index Provider will publis h any changes made to the composition of the Index with sufficient notice before the Rebalance Day.
Extraordinary Rebalance The Index is not rebalanced extraordinarily.
Adjustments Under certain circumstances, an adjustment of the Index may be necessary b etween two regular Rebalance Days.
Such adjustment has to be made if a corporate action (as specified in the guideline of the Index) in relation of an Index component occurs.

Such adjustment may have to be done in relation to an Index constituent and/or may also affect the number of Index constituents and/or the weighting of certain Index constituents and will be made in compliance with the Solactive Equity Index Methodology, which is incorporated by reference and available on the website of the Index Provider: https://www.solactive.com/documents/equity-index-methodology/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).
The Index Provider will announce the Index adjustment giving a notice period of at least two Trading Days (with respect to the affected Index constituents) on the website of the Index Provider.
The Index adjustments will be implemented on the effective day specified in the respective notice.
234 Methodology Review The methodology of the Index is subject to regular review, at least annually.
In case a ne ed of a change of the methodology has been identified within such review (e.g.
if the underlying market or economic reality has changed since the launch of the Index, i.e.

if the present methodology is based on obsolete assumptions and factors and no longe r reflects the reality as accurately, reliably and appropriately as before), such change will be made in accordance with the Solactive Methodology Policy, which is incorporated by reference and available on the website of the Index Provider: https://www.solactive.com/documents/methodology-policy/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).

Such change in the methodology will be announced on the website of the Index Provider under the Section “News”, which is available at : https://www.solactive.com/news/announcements/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).

Index Committee An index committee composed of staff from the Index Provider and its subsidiaries (the “ Index Committee”) is responsible for decisions regarding any amendments to the rules of the Index.

Any such amendment, which may result in an amendment of the guideline of the Index, must be submitted to the Index Committee for prior approval and will be made in complian ce with the methodology policy, which is available on the website of the Index Provider: https://www.solactive.com/documents/methodology-policy/ (this website has not been reviewed by the SFC).
